data.title: Meta: Giving Credit To Zuckerberg's AI Blueprint
data.description:
I only started covering Meta Platforms, Inc. (NASDAQ:META) on Seeking Alpha in May this year, after Q1 earnings confirmed to me this is a winning AI bet.

However, my journey with Meta as an investor actually began in 2022, when the company’s stock crashed after a couple of bad earnings reports, with investors at the time unconvinced about Meta’s CAPEX investment in questionable “Metaverse” bets.

At that time, I entered a relatively small position in Meta worth $ ~5,000, as I was convinced its role in a de facto online Advertising oligopoly all but guaranteed a turnaround. I held that position until it roughly tripled in value before selling most of it. In hindsight, I shouldn’t have sold, as the company went on outperforming the market. That position would have now returned a ~5X from my 2022 entry price.

What happened to determine such a successful turnaround? I believe the answer lies in CEO Mark Zuckerberg’s long-term vision for his company in the AI race. This is what I will cover in this article.

What is Zuckerberg’s AI vision?

During Q2 earnings, Meta’s CEO outlined five areas of opportunity tied to AI: improved advertising, engaging experiences, business messaging, Meta AI advancements and AI devices. In the next paragraphs, I will go through each of these items outlining how Zuckerberg’s vision evolved in the last few years.

For what concerns “improved advertising,” this is Meta’s ability to improve the effectiveness of its advertising solutions. In simple terms, Meta’s proprietary advertising algorithms are able to increasingly serve better Ads to the right people at the right time, resulting in better conversions and higher ROI for advertisers.

More effective advertising drives more spend per advertiser and more advertisers to Meta’s platforms, resulting in higher growth figures.

This effect is compounded by what Zuckerberg calls “engaging experiences.” This amounts to leveraging AI to make sure that more eyeballs stay on Meta’s platform for longer periods of time. The more people spend on Meta’s platforms, the more (ever-more effective) Ads can be served and the better they can be monetized.

Earlier in 2025, Meta’s noted a 5% year-over-year increase in time spent on Facebook and a 6% increase on Instagram, driven by AI recommendation systems and video engagement (over 20% YoY growth in video time spent on both platforms).

From a financial standpoint, results speak for themselves. Meta’s stock returned almost double what the S&P 500 (SP500) and Nasdaq 100 (NDX) did in the last five years. Given that Meta’s revenue is dependent on advertising for 98%+, I believe it is safe to say this performance is the direct result of an Advertising business that has been growing consistently in double digits for a number of years.

Meta has done a great job at leveraging AI to boost its current business - something that is further reflected in Q2 earnings figure, especially for what concerns:

  • Average price per Ad increasing by 9% y-o-y.

  • Overall Ad impressions growing by 11% y-o-y.

All in all, it is clear to me that Meta is increasing both the time people spend on its platforms and the effectiveness of the Ads they are served while there. However, I think the future of the company lies in its other AI bets.

From Meta’s Ads bread and butter to future engines of growth

Business messaging, Meta AI and AI devices are at the core of the future growth of Meta, in my view. The first two are closely intertwined, with Zuckerberg and Meta’s CFO calling out the following new business models for increased monetization:

  • Ads and channel subscriptions roll-out in WhatsApp, a widely used messaging platform with 1.5 Billion users worldwide

  • Growth of advertising in Threads, Meta’s recent social media platform that rivals Elon Musk’s X.

  • Growth of website-to-message ads, directing users to a business’s website before initiating a chat in Meta’s messaging apps.

  • AI-powered business agents that either integrate into ad campaigns or into customers’ e-commerce platforms, likely through paid services or subscriptions.

The latter element is particularly interesting, in my opinion. I think it could enable Meta to have a completely new business model, unrelated to Advertising. Zuckerberg said what follows talking about the future of business AI during the Q2 earnings call (emphasis added):

I’ve talked before about how I believe every business will soon have a business AI, just like they have an e-mail address, social media account and website. We are starting to see some product market fit in a number of countries where we’re testing these agents, and we’re integrating these business AIs into ads on Facebook and Instagram as well as directly into e-commerce websites.

I believe we are still at the early stages of what can be achieved by Meta in terms of leveraging AI. As I discussed in past coverage, I think we are only entering a new AI wave that will be centered on Agentic AI and inference training.

It is in this context that, for the first time, I saw in Meta’s Q2 earnings a possible path to monetizing the large messaging platforms the company has acquired in the past years. More importantly, I saw a path to diversifying the business from Advertising. This is why I am fundamentally bullish on Meta long term, believing Zuckerberg will be able to continue delivering for shareholders.

My bullish sentiment is only compounded by "AI devices," the last element mentioned by Meta's CEO as an area of focus for AI monetization. While we are still in the very early stages, I think this is an interesting bet of Meta on Robotics, which I personally see as the last step in the AI race - with physical AI integrated in our daily lives.

Meta's AR glasses are an interesting take on the world of Augmented Reality. While it is too early to draw conclusions on the TAM for that kind of product (and whether, for example, it could ever replace smartphones), this represents yet another potential future catalyst for the stock.

About Meta’s valuation

At a Forward P/E of ~27 at the time of writing, readers may find Meta expensive. It is true that if compared to other Tech companies, this is a fairly rich valuation. For example, Alphabet Inc. (GOOGL) trades at below a P/E of 20. That, however, is in my view linked to the threat that AI poses to Search. Unlike Meta, Google has not done a great job at convincing it can truly benefit from AI, and is stuck in a more defensive position - something I covered in my recent article on it about how the web is changing.

Yet, Meta trades at a discount against Microsoft Corporation (MSFT), with a FWD P/E of ~33 at the time of writing. And even against Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN), with a similar FWD P/E to that of Microsoft. I think this relative discount is once again due to Meta’s dependence on Advertising. Should the company manage to show a realistic diversification of its business, I believe we could easily see the market pricing in line with Microsoft - representing a ~20% upside from current price levels.

On top of the possibility of a valuation “reset” if it diversifies its business, I expect Meta will keep returning value to shareholders’ organically as a result of accelerated growth in its underlying business. After two quarters of 20%+ revenue growth, I am convinced Meta has unlocked a new pattern of AI-fueled growth.

Overall, I am a strong believer that it is better to pay a premium to buy an excellent business than buying a lousy one at a discount. That’s why I keep rating Meta a BUY, even after a sustained bull run and considering its valuation is at somewhat of a premium. I think it is a no brainer for long term holders who want to be exposed to AI.

Risks

The main risk of an investment in Meta concerns its dependence on Advertising. In case of an economic recession, advertising is usually one of the first industries that are hit as companies cut on their marketing budgets. Meta’s strength - its innovative and highly efficient Ad business - is also its weakness, in that a decline in Advertising spend can hit the business disproportionately when compared to more diversified businesses, such as Microsoft or Amazon.

The company can also be subject to high volatility, being a Tech company that is trading at a fairly rich valuation compared to historical norms (the S&P 500 historical FWD P/E ratio is around 20). I would not recommend a hefty exposure to META for any investor that is not willing to hold long term, and in any case only if such exposure is consistent with the investor’s objectives and risk profile.

Conclusion

In my opinion, Meta’s own brand name is a testament to Mark Zuckerberg’s long term vision for AI. The company was rebranded as Meta in reference to the “Metaverse,” a concept linked to Web3 that was popular in Silicon Valley in the early 2020s. Barely anyone talks about the Metaverse nowadays, with “AI” now used as a catch-all term for anything related to the evolution of the web (including that of social media). I am convinced that if Zuckerberg was to rebrand Meta today, he would choose something AI-related.

Even if the term “AI” was not as used back then as it is today, in the last 4 to 5 years Mark Zuckerberg exhibited what I think amounts to a great long term vision. Results speak for themselves, with an advertising business growing in the 20%s for multiple quarters (far ahead that of Google, its closest competitors in online advertising) and a stock that outperformed both the S&P 500 as well as the Nasdaq 100 in that time frame.

The fact Meta has confirmed they will continue investing in AI via heavy CAPEX allocation is testament to the fact they are walking the talk. I expect Meta to keep delivering value for shareholders going forward, and that’s why I rate it a Buy. Should the company manage not only to keep growing at current rates, but to diversify its business away from Advertising as a result of its AI effort, I would expect the shares to offer outsized returns going forward.

data.title: Dow Jones And Major U.S. Indices Drop From Key Levels - Technical Outlook
data.description:
By Elior Manier

Despite a strong open and a decent Jobless Claims release, Equity markets are rejcting their higher levels. The narrative had been confusing since last Friday's Non-Farm payrolls miss:

All indices rallied above their pre-NFP levels in what seemed to be a total discounting of that new information, allowing participants to suppose that the repricing of a September cut (currently 91.5% priced in for 25 bps) would overtake the more pessimistic outlooks for Economy.Was the few days of price action a bull trap? Only the future will tell.

But one sure thing, is that single days of price action are not enough to look at the global picture, a good reason to always take a step back and look at higher timeframes.It is costly to be bearish on Equities which are growing stronger every month. However, with tariffs now in place and data deceleration, it is essential to approach Markets with more caution and treat risk-management measures with importance.

On Monday, we looked at gold and oil performance showing a different story than stocks—commodities are good indicators of actual economic demand and it's good to look at their evolution.

Markets may also look at the continuing claims being at their highest level since November 2021 – It will be essential to see how this story evolves.Let's take a look at charts from the Dow Jones, S&P 500 and Nasdaq to see what technicals are showing.

Dow Jones 8H Chart

The Dow initially bounced higher from oversold NFP levels all the way back to the July range Pivot Zone (44,400 to 44,500).

These mid-range zones tend to be price magnets for retracements, as lots of volumes get trades there. The rebound stopped at the 38.2% Fib of the move down that started just a week ago, with daily highs at 44,519 (CFD, actual index 44,498). The current 8H candle is a strong bearish one, taking the index right around the middle of its longer run downwards channel – Buyers will want to step in around here to avoid sellers keeping their strong hand. Levels to keep in check :

Resistance Levels

  • 44,100 (+/- 3 points) intraday resistance
  • 44,400 to 44,500 Pivot turned Resistance
  • All-time high resistance zone around 45,000

Support Levels

  • Current Pivot 43,500 to 43,750
  • NFP Lows Mini-Support (43,250
  • 43,000 Main Support Zone
  • 42,000 June post-war Support

S&P 500 8H Chart

The S&P got very close to the 6,400 handle with a 6,389 top at the Daily open but is currently down 70 points from there, and was relatively stronger than its big brother, the Dow, as bulls took it to just above the 23.6% of the NFP down-move. RSI Momentum is becoming bearish and sellers pushed the index below its 8H MA 50.

The down session from two days ago stalled at the 6,300 landmark so it will be essential to watch what the index does when it gets there. Any break below will look to test the NFP lows (6,216). Key levels to place on your charts:

Resistance Levels

  • 6,441 ATH on CFD (6,427 on SPX Index)
  • Opening bell highs 6,404 (CFD, 6,389 on the actual index)
  • FOMC Lows resistance Zone 6,350

Support Levels

  • 6,300 Key Support – Current Pivot (+/- 15 points)
  • NFP Lows and lower bound of May Channel – 6,220 to 6,240
  • 6,152 January ATH level

Nasdaq 8H Chart

The Nasdaq saw the biggest reversal, going from up 0.40% on the session to down 0.22% after rejecting the 23,500 zone. The ongoing selling is currently stalling before the 23,215 50-period MA which will require a bullish impulse to avoid, once again, sellers keeping their current strong hand. One major level to look to hold the higher timeframe uptrend is the lower bound of the May lows ascending channel. RSI momentum seems to be turning lower but is still above the neutral line, a development to keep an eye on. Levels to watch:

Resistance levels

  • 23,732 Current all-time highs
  • 23,500 psychological resistance zone
  • Daily highs 23,578 (CFD, actual index 23,560)

Support levels

  • Pivot Zone at 23,150
  • Lower bound of ascending channel 22,915
  • 22,700 Support

Safe Trades!

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Flowserve: Continuing Alone Without Chart
data.description:
Early in June, I believed that investors in Flowserve (NYSE:FLS) were cautious on the announced merger with Chart Industries (GTLS). Forwarding two months in time, Flowserve´s investors have to focus on the standalone investment case after a competing offering for Chart rightfully resulted in Flowserve calling the merger off, which makes for an interesting reimbursement on its way.

Shares of Flowserve have recovered from the initial negative reaction here, yet amidst uninspiring second quarter results, which are actually indicative of its long-term performance, a fair to low valuation applied to Flowserve does not automatically make me upbeat here.

A Merger Of Equals

In June, both firms announced their intention to merge in what can best be described as a merger of equals. While strategic arguments, which claimed greater diversification and of course synergies, looked good on paper, investors were very cautious. This was evident as shares of both firms fell upon the announcement.

That can be understood as Flowserve, as the surviving entity would see a jump in leverage, while investors in Chart would merge their higher growth prospects into the slower growth prospects of Flowserve. Deal terms called for Flowserve to issue 3.165 shares of its stock for every share outstanding of Chart, which would give investors in Chart a combined 53% equity stake in the business.

By merging Flowserve´s $4.6 billion business, which focuses on pumps, seals, control valves, and Chart's $4.2 billion business, which focuses on heat exchanges, compressors, cryogenic storage, etc., the idea was to create an $8.8 billion diversified industrial energy giant. This business was expected to deliver on $300 million in synergies over time, as Flowserve's margins and growth prospects lagged that of Chart substantially.

Shares of Flowserve fell $3 to $47 per share following the announcement of the deal, as valuations, including the to-be-issued shares, were down about $800 million. Shares of Chart fell some 8% to $148 per share as investors in both firms missed the strategic rationale behind the deal yet failed to have conviction to buy the dip.

Failed Merger, Happy Stock

By the end of July, Flowserve announced that it had terminated the anticipated merger with Chart, this news being communicated alongside the release of the second quarter results.

This followed Chart Industries obtaining an unsolicited acquisition proposal from Baker Hughes (BKR), which was both superior in terms of value and certainty, this being a $210 cash offer. Furthermore, frankly, there seems better overlap between these two firms.

As a result, Flowserve decided not to compete with Baker Hughes, making Flowserve eligible to receive a $266 million termination payment from Chart. With a share count of 131 million shares outstanding, that payment is equal to about $2 per share, yet this is not a free lunch, with the company having incurred expenses as well in relation to the merger, while the business has seen real operational distraction as well.

At the same time, the company reported a near 3% increase in second quarter sales to $1.19 billion, yet organic sales were down a 1% as growth was driven by favorable currency moves, but moreover the impact of acquisitions. However, adjusted margins rose by two points to 14.6% of sales, boosting adjusted earnings by eighteen cents to $0.91 per share.

The gap with GAAP earnings of $0.62 per share is largely driven by costs related to acquisitions, such as amortization and intangible charges, as well as a $0.09 per share charge related to costs incurred with the Chart merger. Somewhat concerning was that bookings only came in at $1.07 billion, and while that ate into the backlog, this backlog of $2.85 billion was still up 6% on the year before.

In terms of the balance sheet, net debt of $856 million was pretty manageable. Second quarter results were somewhat uninspiring from a booking and sales number perspective, although margins were good. This made that the company actually hiked the full-year earnings guidance from $3.10-$3.30 towards $3.25-$3.40 per share. This is even as the higher end of the full-year organic growth guidance was cut by a point to 4%.

Shares have recovered from a deal-induced decline to $47 early in June to $53 at this point in time, driven by a market recovery as well as a hike in the full-year guidance and termination fee receivable. At current levels, multiples look quite fair, with shares trading around 16 times adjusted earnings and the balance sheet being in solid shape.

What Now?

With Flowserve displaying discipline here after a big merger attempt, I like the decisions made by management, as focus on bolt-on dealmaking seems a better idea combined with focus on execution on its own assets. Looking at Flowserve in isolation, we will see a larger competitor if Chart gets sold to Baker Hughes, although that is not really a pure competitor here, so that is not a prime concern.

However, it likely puts pressure on Flowserve to continue to engage at a higher pace in M&A, as Flowserve is simply muddling through here, with a $4.5 billion business in 2024 posting similar sales as it did a decade ago, with investors essentially seeing no growth amidst flattish margins and a flattish share count.

This leaves Flowserve to trade at 16 times adjusted earnings multiples for an earnings yield around 6%, while debt is moderate. This makes it hard to be really upbeat, as the valuations are fair to cheap, but the (past) performance has been uninspiring as well. As a result, I see shares trading in my neutral territory, as I am happy to update the story from here (likely on M&A moves), but the real organic growth pace is what needs addressing here.

data.title: This Market Is Clueless: Why I'm Still All In On Texas Pacific
data.description:
Introduction

There are not many stocks I have mentioned as much as Texas Pacific Land (NYSE:TPL) and LandBridge (LB), which have become my third-largest and largest holdings, respectively.

The reason I'm bringing them up so much isn't to "pump" them, as I doubt I can influence the stock prices of multi-billion dollar companies, anyway, but because they touch on so many things that I'm bullish on.

For example, on July 21, I wrote an article titled "Betting The Ranch: My 2 Largest Investments Just Got Even Better," which discussed a number of secular growth tailwinds, including just how power hungry data centers have become. Between 2020 and 2030, global data center power demand is expected to rise to almost 1,000 TWh, a surge of nearly 5x.

The second tailwind is the one big bottleneck pressuring America's energy industry. That bottleneck is produced water in the Permian Basin. As some of you may know, the Permian Basin is the largest oil basin in the United States.

The basin produces roughly as much oil as all other basins combined.

It's also the only major basin capable of production growth, which explains the widening output gap since the 2020 pandemic, as we can see below.

While the Permian is seeing pressure from falling oil prices and deteriorating Tier 1 reserves, the biggest problem is produced water, which is a byproduct of oil and gas production in the form of toxic water that needs to be dealt with.

In some areas of the Permian (mainly in the Delaware Basin), every barrel of oil comes with up to ten barrels of water. As oil producers are drilling deeper, water is only becoming a bigger problem, leading to the Federal Reserve Bank of Dallas reporting that the majority of oil companies see long-term constraints from this water problem.

Not to sound dramatic, but given that the Permian was the core of the shale revolution that provided the world with nearly unprecedented oil supply growth between the Great Financial Crisis and the pandemic, Permian water is a global bottleneck problem that needs to be taken care of.

As I showed in that article, drillers are willing to pay a lot of money for water disposal, as prices have risen from roughly $0.15 in 2010 to $1.20 in 2025.

The third tailwind is that I consider energy stocks, in general, to be too cheap and a perfect asset in light of "higher for longer" inflation and interest rate risks. Also, given that money managers are massively underweight in energy, I expect a substantial boost the moment we get some support from cyclical growth.

All of this is bullish for Texas Pacific Land and LandBridge.

Unfortunately, their stock price performances this year haven't reflected any of this good news as their stock prices are 50% (for TPL) and 35% (for LB) below their all-time highs. For TPL, it's the third-worst correction (crash?) of the past 10 years, as the chart below visualizes quite well.

While I have a thick skin, I have to say that it annoys me.

Or, as I wrote in many comments, "I'm 100% annoyed but 0% worried."

However, it makes assessing their quarterly numbers even more important, which is why I decided to fully dedicate this article to Texas Pacific and its just-released earnings.

As we have a lot to discuss, let's get right to it!

I Don't Care What The Market Thinks, This Was An Awesome Quarter

I have to admit that the title above may sound like it was written by someone who is now doing whatever it takes to make his investment look good.

However, the truth is different.

After all, I do not benefit from sugarcoating anything. It would only hurt my own net worth, given the size of my TPL investment and tarnish my credibility, something I have spent more than a decade building.

So, let me explain what I mean by this subheader.

For starters, I believe the single-biggest reason why Texas Pacific is down is the low price of oil. Currently, WTI crude oil is trading for $67 per barrel, a price that's substantially off its lows this year but still a number that causes oil executives in the Lone Star State to play defense.

After all, using Dallas Fed data, we can assume most drillers need prices of $70 to $75 to pull the trigger on higher output. While $67 per barrel means Permian producers can profitably drill new wells, the margin is slim.

Also, oil is down due to OPEC-related uncertainty, poor cyclical growth conditions, tariffs impacting drilling costs and economic growth, and general uncertainty surrounding the energy policy of President Donald Trump.

That's why producers have been dropping rigs.

As we can see below, the total rig is roughly 20% below its 2024 average.

So, needless to say, when bears say this is bad for Texas Pacific, they are not wrong. It's a valid argument and one of the biggest risks for TPL, in general.

However, it's just one part of the story.

For example, the rig count is roughly half of what it was in 2019. Despite that, total oil production has consistently made new highs, supported by the ever-increasing efficiency of American onshore drillers.

All of this is reflected in TPL's quarterly numbers as its total water sales dropped to 482 thousand barrels per day. That's down from 800 thousand barrels per day in 2Q24 and a result of lower drilling activity.

The fewer wells are drilled, the less freshwater is needed.

Also, oil realizations dropped to $63.99 per barrel, down from nearly $81.00 per barrel in the second quarter of 2024. This decline is obvious.

Now comes the good news.

Despite all of this bad news, Texas Pacific Land reported $188 million in revenues, a 9% increase compared to 2Q24, when it generated $172 million.

Wait, what?

Higher revenues are not the result of smart financial engineering but because total oil and gas royalty production rose to 33.2 thousand barrels per day. Bear in mind that this is the share of production it gets from drillers on its land. Texas Pacific does not produce a single drop of oil.

It also saw a new record in produced water royalties to 4,248 thousand barrels per day, a substantial increase from 3,321 thousand barrels in 2Q24.

This allowed the company to maintain an EBITDA margin of 89% and 69% free cash flow margins, making it one of the three most efficient S&P 500 companies.

These numbers underscore the extreme resilience of its "customers" (see above) and the quality of the land it owns. While the number of total rigs has dropped, total permits and drilled but uncompleted wells remain strong.

As we can see above, Permian production has done very well in recent years, despite a lower rig count. Going forward, I expect the rig count to remain stable and most upside to come from production efficiencies and higher water volumes (both fresh water sales and produced water royalties).

During its earnings call, the company addressed the notion that the Permian Basin production is nearing a plateau or decline. Management argued that this perception conflates a temporary slowdown due to low oil prices with geological exhaustion.

The Permian, which spans millions of acres across West Texas and New Mexico, remains the world's largest oil and gas basin, producing 6.5 million barrels of oil per day (about half of U.S. oil production) and 3 million barrels per day of natural gas liquids, accounting for 9% of global liquids supply.

While the growth fears I just discussed are valid, there's no reason to suggest TPL will run into trouble anytime soon. We will likely see decades of elevated production, rising water demand, and new opportunities as prices rise.

Sure, it's not as great as it was in 2013, but I cannot imagine an oil basin I would rather invest in than the mighty Permian, especially because Texas Pacific doesn't carry the capex risks that its customers deal with, as it simply does not produce oil and gas (see below).

Moreover, to support my point, the company cited a report by Inveris that estimates more than 60,000 drilling locations with breakevens below $60 WTI and $3 natural gas, which represent more than 30 billion barrels of oil.

At the 2024 drilling pace of 5,700 wells per year, this would translate to an 11-year inventory for low-breakeven wells, with additional locations becoming economic at higher oil prices.

More Growth Opportunities

One of the things I mentioned a few times in this report is advanced drilling.

This includes horseshoe drilling. This method is called a horseshoe because of the U-shaped wells that allow operators to maximize lateral length within constrained leasehold boundaries. Essentially, it reduces their surface area and capital costs.

There's not a lot of info online, as this is a rather new technology. Currently, Texas Pacific Land has 48 horseshoe wells on its land. Three years ago, that number was zero.

Moreover, the Permian is home to tens of thousands of older wells that were all completed with less efficient technologies. All of these could be re-drilled in the future, especially if higher oil prices make it more economically viable.

So far, so good.

Until this point, I have mainly explained why the Permian Basin remains a great place to be and why Texas Pacific Land was able to defy weakness in 2Q25.

I'm even more upbeat about water.

Currently, the Permian produces more than 23 million barrels of produced water every single day, a number that's expected to substantially increase due to maturing wells and an increasingly unfavorable water-to-oil ratio for drillers.

Texas Pacific has three ways to benefit from this:

  • In-basin disposal capacity: TPL's surface acreage retains millions of barrels per day of disposal capacity, with a conservative approach to signing disposal wells to preserve injection capacity in light of increasing regulatory scrutiny.
  • Out-of-basin disposal: TPL has acquired tens of thousands of acres of out-of-basin pore space. Currently, it's injecting more than 100,000 barrels per day, with plans for further growth this number. Going forward, I expect a lot more out-of-basin disposal, which should benefit pipeline easement income, as midstream companies will have to provide the necessary infrastructure on TPL and third-party land.
  • Desalination and beneficial reuse: As I have written in prior articles, Texas Pacific is working on water cleaning technologies. The desalination facility aims to reduce subsurface injection while producing fresh water for industrial uses like power generation, data center cooling, or hydrogen production. Essentially, this way, the company can benefit from water more than three times as it sells fresh water, cleans produced water, and uses this for value-adding projects. While this is a slow process, I'm very bullish on its long-term benefits.

These benefits are often underestimated, as the company, which has not a penny in gross debt and more than $500 million in cash, has doubled its royalty production and sourced water revenue since the start of 2021. During this period, it has tripled its produced water royalty volumes.

The Selloff and Valuation

Currently, TPL is selling off more than 7% while I'm writing this, pushing its stock price below $900 in a move that I blame on uncertainty regarding Permian drilling activity and a misunderstanding of what TPL is all about.

Even after losing half of its value, TPL still trades north of 40x earnings, a number that may seem abysmal to most. However, it's close to its long-term median as it simply deserves a high multiple.

See, on top of not having any debt, it has sky-high margins, as its capex and opex are minimal. It does not produce oil and gas. All it does is allow others to produce on its land and invest in hardware for water sales, among other minor things.

A company trading at 20x earnings can be much more expensive than TPL's 40x multiple as a much bigger share of these earnings will have to be invested in the business again. For TPL, it's almost all free cash flow.

Moreover, going forward, expectations foresee 30% and 18% EPS growth in 2025 and 2026. While these numbers are subject to change, I believe the company has a fair stock price of at least $1,300, based on its current multiple.

As annoying as this oil environment may be, the company has shown what it is capable of. Just imagine what this could mean in an environment where oil creeps up to $85-$90 again.

The future for TPL is bright.

Takeaway

I'm not sugarcoating it. Watching Texas Pacific Land and LandBridge, which I will cover tomorrow, tumble 50% and 35% from their highs is beyond frustrating.

But here's the thing: I think the market is wrong. It usually is during times of falling oil prices. While some weakness is justified, this crash is not.

TPL just reported a quarter that proves its resilience, with higher revenues despite lower oil prices, record water royalties, and sky-high margins (89% EBITDA, 69% FCF).

Even better, the Permian Basin isn't dying. If anything, it's adapting.

Drillers are squeezing more from fewer rigs, and water demand is exploding, which I expect to continue for many years to come.

While oil at $67 hurts, TPL isn't an oil producer, but a landowner with zero debt and more than $500 million in cash.

At 40x earnings, it may not seem cheap. However, unless you believe oil will never recover, I consider it to be a very cheap stock, which is why I stick to a Strong Buy rating.

Risk to My Thesis

The biggest risk to my thesis is a "lower for longer" oil price environment. It would cause oil companies to become even more defensive and TPL's growth to slow. Regulatory risks are subdued, as most plans already foresee more water shipments to out-of-basin spots.

Financial risks are subdued, as TPL has no debt and no long-term contracts with third parties that could hurt it in any way.

Hence, as I said, I'm 100% annoyed but 0% worried.

data.title: The Case For The ERX ETF Product
data.description:
In a late April 2025 Seeking Alpha article on the Direxion Daily 2X Shares ETF (NYSEARCA:ERX), I concluded:

For those looking to buy a dip or sell a rally in the crude oil and oil-related shares, ERX and ERY are valuable tools. However, the leverage requires any risk position to include a risk-reward plan, incorporating time and price stops to avoid the painful impact of time decay.

I expect two-way volatility in crude oil and oil-related shares to continue as markets reflect the economic and geopolitical landscapes, which remain turbulent as we head into May 2025 after a very volatile April.

Crude oil and oil-related company share prices have been trending higher since the April 9 low. ERX was trading at the $48.75 level on April 24, 2025. The leveraged ETF was higher in early August 2025, but requires time and price stops as it suffers from time decay, which weighs on its value if the traditional energy-related shares remain stable or decline.

Oil prices are steady

Nearby NYMEX crude oil prices have traded in a range of $55.12 to $79.39 per barrel in 2025.

The daily continuous contract NYMEX WTI crude oil futures chart highlights this year’s price action in the leading energy commodity. The 2025 high was reached on January 15, with the low occurring on April 9, following the Trump administration’s rollout of unprecedented tariffs on its global trading partners. Meanwhile, hostilities in the Middle East lifted the price to a slightly lower high of $78.40 on June 23, 2025, at which point the WTI futures ran out of upside steam. At the $64.25 level on August 7, nearby NYMEX crude oil futures were just below the midpoint of the 2025 trading range. While prices have declined from the late June peak, they have been steady between the $63 and $70 level since late June.

Strength in distillate crack spreads reflects the Middle East risks

Crack spreads can be useful metrics as they serve as real-time demand indicators for oil products, gasoline, and distillates. The processing spreads reflect the margin refiners receive for processing a barrel of crude oil into oil products.

The weekly chart of the NYMEX gasoline shows that at around the $23 per barrel level, gasoline crack spreads are seasonally elevated during the peak driving season, when automobile fuel demand is high due to the summer vacation.

Heating oil futures are a proxy for other distillates, including diesel and jet fuels, so the distillate crack spreads display less seasonality than the gasoline processing spreads. At the $31.50 per barrel level in early August, the distillate refining spreads are at an elevated level.

The crack spreads display robust demand for oil products in August 2025, translating to demand for crude oil, which is the primary ingredient in these oil products. Steady prices are a function of the product demand.

Oil companies are doing well - A bullish trend in the XLE ETF

While NYMEX crude oil prices are trading below the midpoint of the 2025 trading range, the S&P 500 Energy Sector SPDR (XLE) is trading near or above its year’s midpoint.

The XLE ETF owns shares of the leading U.S. integrated oil companies and those related to the traditional energy sector. The XLE has over a 41% exposure to ExxonMobil (XOM) and Chevron (CVX), the two leading U.S. integrated oil giants. As the chart highlights, the XLE ETF has traded in a 2025 range of $74.49 to $94.82 per share. At just over the $84.50 level, the XLE is slightly above the midpoint as oil companies have outperformed the NYMEX WTI futures contract for the raw energy commodity.

ERX is the XLE on steroids

The fund summary for the Direxion Daily Energy Bull 2X Shares ETF (ER) states:

ERX is a bullish leveraged ETF that employs options and swaps to turbocharge the XLE’s performance. ERX’s most recent top holdings include:

The chart shows that ERX’s top holdings are XOM and CVX, with many of the same constituents as the XLE. The difference lies in the gearing that enables ERX to outperform the XLE on the upside. However, that outperformance comes at a steep price, with its underperformance on the downside. Meanwhile, when the XLE’s price remains stable, time decay erodes ERX’s value. Therefore, leveraged ETFs, such as ERX, are trading tools, not investment vehicles, requiring price and time stops.

At $50.67 per share, ERX had over $206.4 million in assets under management. ERX trades an average of nearly 449,000 shares daily and charges a 0.90% expense ratio. ERX’s dividend of $1.48 per share translates to a 2.92% yield, which is lower than the XLE’s 3.38% yield.

The XLE rallied 20% from $74.49 on April 9 to $89.43 on June 23. At the $84.60 level on August 7, the XLE was 5.40% lower than its high on June 23.

The turbocharged bullish ERX product rallied 42.4% from $40.60 on April 9 to $57.81 per share on June 23. At $50.77 on August 7, ETX was 12.18% lower.

Poor Seeking Alpha ETF grades, but an excellent short-term trading tool

ERX is an excellent trading tool for experienced market participants who are comfortable handling leverage risks. The price of oversized rewards on the upside is oversized losses on the downside.

The chart highlights Seeking Alpha’s ETF grades for the ERX ETF product.

The most important grade for a leveraged product is its liquidity grade because execution ease is critical for short-term trading tools. ERX receives a B+ in liquidity. Meanwhile, the recent bearish trend and risk of the gearing earn failing grades. The high expense ratio gets a D-, while the lower yield than the XLE ETF receives a D+ in dividends.

ERX is a valuable trading tool for market participants expecting bullish price action in the XLE. ERX’s complimentary bearish product, ERY, delivers turbocharged results when the XLE trends lower.

Steady oil prices in August 2025, and the U.S. administration’s support for fossil fuels through its “drill-baby-drill” and “frack-baby-frack” policies, support U.S. oil and oil-related companies’ earnings. Trading these volatile companies using ERX and ERY with time and price stops could generate significant results.

data.title: Ariel Global Fund Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Performance data quoted represents past performance. Past performance does not guarantee future results. All performance assumes the reinvestment of dividends and capital gains and represents returns of the Investor Class shares. The investment return and principal value of an investment will fluctuate so that an investor's shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than their original cost. Current performance may be lower or higher than the performance data quoted. Performance data current to the most recent month-end for Ariel Global Fund may be obtained by visiting our website, arielinvestments.com. For the period ended June 30, 2025 the average annual total returns of Ariel Global Fund (Investor Class) for the 1, 5, and 10-year periods were +17.61%, +10.00% and +7.26%, respectively.

The second quarter of 2025 was a period of extremes. Stocks tumbled in early April as the “Liberation Day” tariff announcement pushed the MSCI ACWI and S&P 500 to the brink of bear market territory. Yet, after the initial plan was put on “pause,” equities staged a remarkable recovery.

Investor enthusiasm for artificial intelligence (AI) themed stocks and robust corporate earnings results drove global and U.S. indices to new all-time highs. European equities outperformed as a strong euro boosted purchasing power and lifted investor sentiment. Japan also recorded strong gains, while China posted a modest rebound. Yet, beneath the surface, capital flows continued to signal a shifting investment landscape. A weaker dollar, resilient commodities and renewed appetite for emerging markets and European stocks are reshaping portfolios. Investors with a mix of U.S. equities, international exposure and hard assets appear to be better cushioned against elevated risks, including tariffs and geopolitical concerns. While near term volatility may persist, we remain confident that our portfolio companies can navigate through uncertainty. Against this backdrop, Ariel Global Fund traded +7.38% higher in the quarter, falling short of the MSCI ACWI Index’s return of +11.53%, but outpacing the MSCI ACWI Value Index’s +5.84% gain.

Ariel’s non-consensus approach seeks to identify undervalued, out-of-favor, franchises that are misunderstood and therefore mispriced. Ariel Global Fund is overweight Health Care, Utilities, Consumer Discretionary and Communication Services, meaningfully underweight Information Technology, Industrials and Consumer Staples, as well as lacks exposure to Energy and Materials. At the sector level, our avoidance of Energy and stock selection among Consumer Staples were the greatest contributors to returns, while currency forwards, Consumer Discretionary and Industrials holdings were the largest performance detractors.

Leading food retailer and wholesaler based in the United Kingdom (UK), Tesco PLC (OTCPK:TSCDF)(OTCPK:TSCDY), advanced during the period with continued market share gains. Although increased price competition resulted in management lowering full year 2026 guidance, Tesco remains focused on utilizing its scale to protect and grow market share. In our view, Tesco has a good track record of navigating difficult markets and should continue to benefit from alternative revenue streams such as Marketplace and Retail Media.

Korea-based leading pure-play producer of memory semiconductor products, SK Hynix Inc. (OTCPK:HXSCF) also traded higher following robust quarterly earnings results. Strong AI driven demand for high bandwidth memory (HBM) products as well as a recovery in commodity memory prices were key highlights. Furthermore, the company is gaining market share in high-content server-grade dynamic random access memory (DRAM) modules and solid state drives (SSDs). With its entire 2025 HBM supply sold out and Nvidia as a key customer, SK Hynix solidified its leadership in the AI memory market.

New holding, Lasertec Corporation (OTCPK:LSRCY)(OTCPK:LSRCF), which is a Japan-based niche manufacturer of semiconductor and flat panel display production equipment, was another top contributor over the period. A recent pullback in the stock following order cuts, product inspection delays and concerns about the threat of new competitors provided an attractive entry point. Following our initiation, improving investor sentiment lifted shares as Lasertec remains the only company with commercialized inspection systems that use extreme ultraviolet lithography (EUV) light sources. Management also reiterated their expectation for an order recovery in 2026 with the number of specific projects increasing and visibility in the outlook improving. Moreover, we believe Lasertec’s process control intensity is nearing an inflection as the business shifts towards high volume manufacturing. Notably, the company’s actinic patterned mask inspection (APMI) tool also provides a cheaper alternative to advanced semiconductor materials lithography (ASML). We think this in combination with an expansion of EUV processes in semiconductor production offers significant upside over the long-term.

Alternatively, China-based e-commerce company, JD.com, Inc. (JD)(OTCPK:JDCMF) declined during the quarter primarily due to concerns surrounding the company's aggressive expansion into the highly competitive food delivery business. Specifically, the magnitude of investment and incremental return on the initiative remains unclear. Meanwhile, the core e-commerce business continues to deliver strong results highlighted by double-digit top line growth and margin expansion. Although the valuation remains attractive and the core business is doing well, we have trimmed the position to reflect our emerging concerns.

New holding, China Mengniu Dairy Company, Ltd. (OTCPK:CIADF)(OTCPK:CIADY), one of the largest dairy producers in China, also traded lower over the period. Recently an oversupply of raw milk has led to falling prices and a loss of market share. Despite this near-term challenge, we expect a healthier supply-demand balance will pave the way for top-line recovery and market share gains. In parallel, Mengniu will work to improve profitability through various self-help initiatives, including tighter expense management and enhanced supply chain efficiency.

Lastly, French pharmaceutical company, Sanofi (SNY)(OTCPK:SNYNF) underperformed during the period, primarily due to renewed concerns over potential U.S. tariffs on European pharmaceutical imports. While the company continues to demonstrate strength in its drug pipeline, mixed results from Phase 3 candidate Itepekimab in recent chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) clinical trials further weighed on shares. At its current valuation, we believe Sanofi's immunology pipeline is underappreciated and are awaiting clinical Phase 3 outcomes in Amlitelimab for atopic dermatitis. Additionally, we remain optimistic in the growth trajectory of Dupixent.

In addition to Lasertec Corporation and China Mengniu Dairy Company Ltd. discussed above, we initiated seven new positions in the quarter.

We added AirTAC International Group, a Taiwan-based pneumatic equipment manufacturer. Pneumatic systems are an attractive power output and given Chinese stimulus, we expect growing demand from electronics, auto and battery, as well as general industrials to trend higher. Notably, AirTAC has been steadily gaining share in the region through its direct sales efforts, new SKU launches, lower prices and improving product quality. Taken together, we expect these competitive advantages will yield attractive long-term earnings growth.

We bought Spain-based global commercial bank Banco Bilbao Vizcaya Argentaria SA (BBVA)(OTCPK:BBVXF) which operates the largest bank in Mexico and has leading franchises across South America and Turkey. We expect loan growth in Mexico to be driven by market share gains, low levels of leverage and the potential near shoring of US production. We also favor BBVA’s exposure to Turkey and its attractive age demographics. Falling inflation and a reduction in the policy rate in the country should help revenues prosper. A potential merger between BBVA and Banco Sabadell would also unlock significant value via market share gains, cost synergies and scale in product offerings as well as technological investments. Even though BBVA offers a superior return-on- equity relative to peers, at current levels shares are trading at a meaningful discount.

We purchased Ebara Corporation (OTCPK:EBCOF)(OTCPK:EBCOY), a Japan-based manufacturer of industrial and semiconductor-related equipment. We believe Ebara’s Precision Machinery division is well positioned to benefit from a near-term recovery in memory wafer fab equipment (WFE) spending, particularly in NAND. Longer term, the adoption of hybrid bonding should increase demand for Ebara’s chemical mechanical planarization (CMP) tools. Lastly, we favorably view the new management team’s plan to narrow its margin gap with peers through operating leverage, expanding its service mix and optimizing its global footprint.

We also added First Solar, Inc. (FSLR), a U.S.-based photovoltaic solar technology and manufacturing company. As the only US based solar module manufacturer of scale, we believe the company stands to benefit from growing AI power demands as well as “America First” energy policy tailwinds. As of today, FSLR is sold out through 2026. However, as the final Inflation Reduction Act rules become clearer, we believe FSLR’s contract wins should accelerate, particularly as developers seek to secure as much capacity as possible to pre- qualify modules for subsidies before they expire.

We purchased leading gaming operator in Italy, Lottomatica Group SpA (OTCPK:LTMGF). The company operates top brands such as GoldBet, Better, BetFlag, Lottomatica, and Planetwin365 and seeks to broaden its player base by scaling the business model via an omnichannel approach. With online migration gaining momentum, Lottomatica is growing its competitive advantage by building out its in-house tech stack and cross-brand AI platform. These initiatives are designed to improve operational efficiency, and customer engagement while supporting scalable growth. The recent award of a new Italian online gaming concession is expected to be a significant catalyst, enabling market consolidation and increasing market share.

With Italy’s online gaming penetration well below the average of comparable European markets (33% versus 60%), we believe Lottomatica is well positioned to capitalize on the sector’s digital transformation and long-term growth potential.

We also bought Walt Disney Company (DIS), the storied entertainment conglomerate whose businesses span media networks, film studios, theme parks, cruise ships and consumer products. A recent pullback in the stock provided an attractive entry point. In our view, growth in Disney+ subscribers, higher average revenue per user (APRU), margin expansion in the streaming segment and greater license sales amid successful releases will result in substantial operating income growth, bolstering the company’s overall margins and free cash flow generation.

Finally, we added Zimmer Biomet Holdings, Inc. (ZBH), maker of hips and knees. The company has struggled to maintain market share following its 2015 acquisition of Biomet, but we expect new product launches to deliver mid- single digit top-line growth over the next three-to-five years. In success ZBH should experience incremental revenue and earnings growth as well as margin expansion.

Meanwhile, we successfully exited biopharmaceutical company, AbbVie, Inc. (ABBV), French banking and financial services company, BNP Paribas SA (OTCQX:BNPQF)(OTCQX:BNPQY) and banking and insurance services company, KBC Group NV (OTCPK:KBCSF)(OTCPK:KBCSY) on valuation. In order to pursue more compelling opportunities, we eliminated our positions in smartphones maker, Samsung Electronics Company, Ltd. (OTCPK:SSNLF); agriculture machinery manufacturer, CNH Industrial NV (CNH); Stellantis N.V. (STLA), the auto company; and Subaru Corporation (OTCPK:FUJHY)(OTCPK:FUJHF), the Japan-based car maker.

It’s impossible to control tariff policies, global trade negotiations and/or how geopolitics and diverging monetary policy may reverberate across the economic landscape. As such, in times like these, we remain measured, deliberate and actively patient—with a willingness to take advantage of volatility with an eye towards long-term gains.

While corporate earnings have largely remained resilient, mounting margin pressures and signs of consumer fatigue are surfacing, especially across discretionary sectors. Meanwhile, market leadership remains highly concentrated in a narrow group of mega-cap technology stocks, heightening the risk of abrupt reversals if sentiment cools. Against this backdrop, thoughtful active management can deliver significant value. Our international and global holdings currently trade at compelling valuations relative to their growth prospects, while maintaining the financial strength needed for operation resilience. We believe our focus on companies with solid balance sheets, durable earnings, and more diversified exposure will prove defensive and be the key differentiator to outperforming over the long run.

Investments in non-U.S. securities may underperform and may be more volatile than comparable U.S. stocks because of the risks involving non-U.S. economies, markets, political systems, regulatory standards, currencies and taxes. The use of currency derivatives and exchange-traded funds (ETFs) may increase investment losses and expenses, and create more volatility. Investments in emerging markets present additional risks, such as difficulties in selling on a timely basis and at an acceptable price. The intrinsic value of the stocks in which the Fund invests may never be recognized by the broader market. The Fund is often concentrated in fewer sectors than its benchmarks, and its performance may suffer if these sectors underperform the overall stock market. Investing in equity stocks is risky and subject to the volatility of the markets.

As of February 1, 2025, Ariel Global Fund Investor Class had an annual net expense ratio of 1.14% and an annual gross expense ratio of 1.53%. Currently, an expense ratio cap of 1.13% is in place for the Investor Class to waive fees and reimburse certain expenses that exceed this cap. Ariel Investments LLC (the Advisor) is contractually obligated to maintain this expense ratio cap through 1/31/26. The net expense ratio for the Investor Class does not correlate to the Expense Cap due to the inclusion of acquired fund fees and certain other expenses which are excluded from the Expense Cap.

The opinions expressed are current as of the date of this commentary but are subject to change. The information provided in this commentary does not provide information reasonably sufficient upon which to base an investment decision and should not be considered a recommendation to purchase or sell any particular security. There is no guarantee that any of the views expressed will come to fruition or any investment will perform as described.

As of 6/30/2025, Tesco PLC constituted 3.0% of Ariel Global Fund; SK Hynix, Inc. 1.0%; Lasertec Corporation 1.9%; JD.com, Inc. 1.4%; China Mengniu Dairy Company, Ltd. 1.7%; Sanofi 1.9%; Airtac International Group 1.1%; Banco Bilbao Vizcaya Argentaria SA 1.1%; Ebara Corporation 1.8%; First Solar, Inc. 2.7%; Lottomatica Group SpA 1.0%; Walt Disney Company 1.0%; Zimmer Biomet Holdings, Inc. 1.3%; Abbvie, Inc. 0.0%; BNP Paribas S.A. 0.0%; CNH Industrial NV 0.0%; KBC Group N.V. 0.0%; Samsung Electronics Co., Ltd. 0.0%; Stellantis N.V. 0.0%; Subaru Corporation 0.0%.

Portfolio holdings are subject to change. The performance of any single portfolio holding is no indication of the performance of other portfolio holdings of Ariel Global Fund.

A glossary of financial terms provided herein may be found on our website at www.arielinvestments.com.

Indexes are unmanaged. An investor cannot invest directly in an index. The MSCI ACWI (All Country World Index) Index is an equity index of large and mid-cap representation across 23 Developed Markets (DM) and 24 Emerging Markets (EM) countries. Its inception date is January 1, 2001. The MSCI ACWI Value Index captures large and mid-cap securities exhibiting overall value style characteristics across 23 Developed Markets countries and 24 Emerging Markets (EM) countries. Its inception date is December 8, 1997. All MSCI Index net returns reflect the reinvestment of income and other earnings, including the dividends net of the maximum withholding tax applicable to non-resident institutional investors that do not benefit from double taxation treaties.

MSCI uses the maximum tax rate applicable to institutional investors, as determined by the company’s country of incorporation. MSCI makes no express or implied warranties or representations and shall have no liability whatsoever with respect to any MSCI data contained herein. The MSCI data may not be further redistributed or used to create indices or financial products. This report is not approved or produced by MSCI.

Investors should consider carefully the investment objectives, risks, and charges and expenses before investing. For a current summary prospectus or full prospectus which contains this and other information about the funds offered by Ariel Investment Trust, call us at 800-292-7435 or visit our website, arielinvestments.com. Please read the summary prospectus or full prospectus carefully before investing. Distributed by Ariel Distributors LLC, a wholly owned subsidiary or Ariel Investments LLC. Ariel Distributors, LLC is a member of the Securities Investor Protection Corporation.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: AAII Sentiment Survey: Pessimism Spikes
data.description:
Pessimism among individual investors about the short-term outlook for stocks increased in the latest AAII Sentiment Survey. Meanwhile, optimism and neutral sentiment decreased.

Bullish sentiment, expectations that stock prices will rise over the next six months, decreased 5.5 percentage points to 34.9%. Bullish sentiment is below its historical average of 37.5% for the second time in six weeks.

Neutral sentiment, expectations that stock prices will stay essentially unchanged over the next six months, decreased 4.8 percentage points to 21.9%. Neutral sentiment is below its historical average of 31.5% for the 55th time in 57 weeks.

Bearish sentiment, expectations that stock prices will fall over the next six months, increased 10.2 percentage points to 43.2%. Bearish sentiment is unusually high and is above its historical average of 31.0% for the 36th time in 38 weeks.

The bull-bear spread (bullish minus bearish sentiment) decreased 15.7 percentage points to –8.4%. The bull-bear spread is below its historical average of 6.5% for the 25th time in 27 weeks.

This week’s special question asked AAII members what they think about the Federal Reserve’s decision to keep interest rates unchanged.

Here is how they responded:

  • They should have raised rates: 1.0%
  • It was the right move: 66.5%
  • They should have cut rates: 29.6%
  • Not sure/no opinion: 2.6%

This week’s Sentiment Survey results:

  • Bullish: 34.9%, down 5.5 percentage points
  • Neutral: 21.9%, down 4.8 percentage points
  • Bearish: 43.2%, up 10.2 percentage points

Historical averages:

  • Bullish: 37.5%
  • Neutral: 31.5%
  • Bearish: 31.0%

The AAII Sentiment Survey has been conducted weekly since July 1987.

data.title: Ariel Focus Fund Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Performance data quoted represents past performance. Past performance does not guarantee future results. All performance assumes the reinvestment of dividends and capital gains, and represents returns of the Investor Class shares. The investment return and principal value of an investment will fluctuate so that an investor’s shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than their original cost. Current performance may be lower or higher than the performance data quoted. Performance data current to the most recent month end for Ariel Focus Fund may be obtained by visiting our website, arielinvestments.com. For the period ended June 30, 2025 the average annual returns of Ariel Focus Fund (Investor Class) for the 1, 5, and 10 year periods were +10.03%, +11.62%, and +6.63%, respectively.

The second quarter of 2025 was a period of extremes. Stocks tumbled in early April as the “Liberation Day” tariff announcement pushed the S&P 500 to the brink of bear market territory. Yet, after the initial plan was put on “pause,” equities staged a remarkable recovery. Reignited enthusiasm for artificial intelligence (AI) themed stocks, solid economic data and robust corporate earnings drove U.S. indices to new all-time highs. Growth beat value and large caps continued to outperform their small cap brethren. Although policy uncertainty and rising geopolitical risks will likely result in heightened volatility, we continue to view macroeconomic developments through the lens of our long-term investment horizon. Against this backdrop, Ariel Focus Fund traded +4.75% higher in the quarter, outperforming the +3.79% gain posted by Russell 1000 Value Index and underperforming the S&P 500 Index’s +10.94% return.

Shares of global leader in enterprise software, Oracle Corporation (ORCL), accelerated following a significant top- and bottom-line beat and impressive full year 2026 outlook driven by accelerating demand for its cloud and AI capabilities. This supports our view that ORCL’s positioning as the leading provider of database software and cloud-based infrastructure is entrenched, making it a key beneficiary of global demand for generative AI development.

Producer and marketer of crop nutrients, Mosaic Co. (MOS), also traded higher following the delivery of solid quarterly earnings results. The company expects phosphate markets to remain tight through 2025 supported by limited new supply, lower inventories and a reduction in imports driven by tariffs. MOS remains focused on cost discipline, improved free cash flow generation and maintaining an investment grade credit profile, while continuing to return significant capital to shareholders. Given management’s renewed discipline on capital allocation, we continue to believe the company is well positioned.

Additionally, supplier of residential thermal, comfort and security solutions, Resideo Technologies, Inc. (REZI) advanced following solid quarterly earnings results highlighted by organic revenue growth and margin expansion. Synergies from the integration of Snap One are also ahead of expectations. Meanwhile, REZI expects to substantially mitigate any headwinds from tariffs by increasing prices, repositioning inventory and by running its factories at different utilization rates. We believe REZI’s earnings potential is underappreciated. The company is entering a new phase of sustainable growth driven by a secular preference for more connected smart home solutions and product innovation.

In contrast, leading manufacturer of consumer food products, J.M. Smucker Co. (SJM) declined during the period. Quarterly earnings benefitted from the company’s legacy businesses anchored by the Uncrustables brand and coffee portfolio, however continued challenges at Hostess weighed on results. Meanwhile, management delivered disappointing fiscal year 2026 guidance due to expected profit declines driven by inflation related to higher expected coffee costs, tariffs and continued weakness at Hostess. SJM remains focused on stabilizing revenue and management believes SKU and display rationalization alongside marketing investments will help. Longer-term, we continue to believe SJM’s portfolio of iconic and emerging foods brands, coupled with its broad- based innovation and productivity agenda, supports an attractive total shareholder return opportunity.

Oil and natural gas explorer, APA Corporation (APA) also detracted from relative performance in the quarter amid poor investor sentiment across the energy sector due to concerns of slowing global growth. The company continues to execute on its cost reduction plan and announced the divestiture of its Permian Basic assets in New Mexico to paydown debt.

Management remains laser-focused on increasing the operational efficiency of the Callon assets, free cash flow generation and returning capital to shareholders. We believe the company trades at a significant discount to its intrinsic value.

Lastly, oil services company, Core Laboratories, Inc. (CLB) declined in the quarter on mixed earnings results. Although increased demand for diagnostic services in U.S. onshore and offshore markets aided the Production Enhancement segment, this growth was neutralized by weaker-than-expected Reservoir Description performance due to seasonality, geopolitical tensions, tariff concerns and commodity price volatility. Importantly, management noted tariffs should not have a significant impact on the business given its service- related revenue. CLB’s product sales are also primarily manufactured and consumed domestically. Looking ahead, the company continues to project international growth from projects in the Middle East, Asia Pacific and West Africa and remains laser focused on generating positive free cash flow, reducing debt and improving its return on invested capital.

Finally, we bought shares of a company held in other Ariel portfolios during the quarter, Generac Holdings, Inc. (GNRC), a leading global manufacturer of power generation equipment. The company has an unmatched distribution network, product portfolio and enjoys strong brand advantages. We believe this creates a wide moat for this niche business which commands around 80% market share in the North American residential market. Historically, growth has been limited due to a lack of awareness of the benefits of a home standby generator, as well as its high price point. More recently, however, more frequent weather-related and aging infrastructure related power outages have tipped the scales of awareness in both the residential and commercial markets.

This awareness should continue to drive growth, margin expansion and free cash flow generation. The stock came under pressure due to concerns that tariffs and a weakening consumer environment would be penalized near-term demand, giving us an opportunity to acquire shares in a unique, high- quality business.

We did not exit any positions in the quarter.

It’s impossible to control tariff policies, global trade negotiations and/or how geopolitics may reverberate across the economic landscape. As such, in times like these, we remain measured, deliberate and actively patient—with a willingness to take advantage of volatility with an eye towards long-term gains. Our current pro-cyclical positioning reflects our bottom-up conviction in undervalued businesses rather than macroeconomic prognostication.

In our view, underlying U.S. growth appears healthy, and we expect corporate profits will prove resilient. Although interest rates will likely remain “higher for longer,” tax cuts and deregulation should support the broadening of the current market rally beyond the AI trade. Consensus earnings estimates across a variety of sectors suggest the Russell 2000 Index could significantly outpace the Russell 1000 Index over each of the next two years 1. Meanwhile, small cap stocks are trading at their biggest discount to large cap shares since the dot-com boom 1. Once the performance gap between mega-cap growth stocks and smaller company counterparts narrows, we believe our portfolios should be rewarded.

Investing in equity stocks is risky and subject to the volatility of the markets. Investing in small and mid cap companies is riskier and more volatile than investing in large cap companies. The intrinsic value of the stocks in which the Fund invests may never be recognized by the broader market. Ariel Focus Fund is a non-diversified fund and therefore may be subject to greater volatility than a more diversified investment. The Fund is often concentrated in few sectors than its benchmarks, and its performance may suffer if these sectors underperform the overall stock market.

As of February 1, 2025, Ariel Focus Fund Investor Class had an annual net expense ratio of 1.00% and an annual gross expense ratio of 1.18%. Currently, an expense ratio cap of 1.00% is in place for the Investor Class to waive fees and reimburse certain expenses that exceed this cap. Ariel Investments LLC (the Advisor) is contractually obligated to maintain this expense ratio cap through 1/31/26. The net expense ratio for the Investor Class does not correlate to the Expense Cap due to the inclusion of acquired fund fees and certain other expenses which are excluded from the Expense Cap.

The opinions expressed are current as of the date of this commentary but are subject to change. The information provided in this commentary does not provide information reasonably sufficient upon which to base an investment decision and should not be considered a recommendation to purchase or sell any particular security. There is no guarantee that any of the views expressed will come to fruition or any investment will perform as described.

As of 6/30/25, Oracle Corporation constituted 5.0% of Ariel Focus Fund; Mosaic Company 4.0%; Resideo Technologies, Inc. 5.3%; J.M. Smucker Company 4.4%; APA Corporation 4.0%; Core Laboratories, Inc. 1.8% and Generac Holdings, Inc. 3.7%. Portfolio holdings are subject to change. The performance of any single portfolio holding is no indication of the performance of other portfolio holdings of Ariel Focus Fund.

A glossary of financial terms provided herein may be found on our website at www.arielinvestments.com.

Index returns reflect the reinvestment of income and other earnings. Indexes are unmanaged, and investors cannot invest directly in an index. The Russell 1000® Value Index measures the performance of the large-cap value segment of the U.S. equity universe. data.title: Cheniere Energy, Inc. 2025 Q2 - Results - Earnings Call Presentation
data.description: The following slide deck was published by Cheniere Energy, Inc. in conjunction with their 2025 Q2 earnings call.

data.title: Berkshire Hathaway: Rare Misses Are Causing Short-Term Pain
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Berkshire Hathaway Inc. (NYSE:BRK.A, BRK.B) reported their Q2 earnings over the weekend, and it was disappointing given their high standards. Operating profit declined 3.8% YoY, and management shared a cautious forecast, citing global trade conditions and geopolitical instability.

Berkshire's stock price declined afterwards, despite advances across the rest of the market.

The results are certainly discouraging at the moment, but I am not too concerned about the company's long term trajectory. It's hard to bet against CEO Warren Buffett when he is backed by a track record spanning several decades.

However, I thought it would be interesting to analyze the rare misses in the current portfolio of Berkshire Hathaway. Not just one, there are two: Kraft Heinz (KHC) and Occidental Petroleum (OXY).

These two investments have been bad ones for Warren Buffett, and they have been dragging down the entire portfolio.

I will go over the Q2 earnings of Berkshire Hathaway first, and then share my thoughts on Kraft Heinz and Occidental Petroleum.

Earnings... Actually Not Too Bad

The headline of the 2Q earnings was disappointing, with a YoY decline of operating income. However, looking at the quarterly report details, I believe the earnings result is not too bad.

It is true that insurance underwriting income declined substantially compared to last year, but the decline mainly came from a single event (Southern California wildfires in 2025). So, I would say the underwriting income decline is a one time thing, and not any indication of continued poor business performance.

The rest of their businesses actually showed the growth. BNSF's profit grew from $1.2 B in 2024 to $1.4 B in 2025, and Berkshire Hathaway Energy's profit also grew from $655 M in 2024 to $702 M in 2025. Manufacturing, service, and retailing businesses also performed well with income growing from $3.4 B in 2024 to $3.6 B in 2025.

Overall, I would say Berkshire Hathaway is doing well, except for one unfortunate event.

The SEC filing indicated that the lower fuel expense was the major factor for the higher income of BNSF. I expect the lower fuel environment will continue to be the case for the next couple of years with an economy slowdown and OPEC output increase. Therefore, I expect the BNSF and manufacturing to outperform in the shorter term.

As long as we won't have another catastrophic natural disaster that haunts their insurance business, I believe Berkshire Hathaway's operating income will come back around in the long run.

What Went Wrong with Kraft Heinz

Berkshire Hathaway took a $3.8 B write-down related to the Kraft Heinz investment, and, unfortunately, I don't think the stock will turn around anytime soon. Let's talk about what went wrong with Kraft Heinz.

In 2015, 3G Capital and Berkshire Hathaway teamed up to broker the merger of H.J. Heinz company and Kraft Food Group. Warren Buffett and company thought the merger will create large synergies and reward shareholders. Improved economies of scale was estimated to create $1.5 B per year of cost savings, while the international growth of Kraft's brands, using Heinz's global footprint, was going to bring huge revenue growth.

Unfortunately, this grand plan didn't really pan out. The revenue of Kraft-Heinz has stayed flat since 2016 at $26 B, and the profit has bounced between $8 B and $10 B over the past 10 years.

The struggle was caused by multiple factors. The first one relates to changing consumer preferences. In the past decade, the consumer has been trending toward healthier eating habits, and moving away from processed food like Lunchables, macaroni and cheese, and Capri Sun that are core products of Kraft Heinz.

The rise of private label also hurt Kraft Heinz. Over the past couple of decades, big retailers like Walmart (WMT) and Costco (COST) have started offering private labels, which has been encroaching into Kraft Heinz's market share. Also, these cheaper private label brands have put price pressure on Kraft Heinz, compressing their margin.

Additionally, the aggressive cost cutting measures implemented by 3G Capital resulted in not enough investment for new products. Maximizing profit instead of building up the business has hurt their growth trajectory.

Overall, the macro trend toward healthier and organic food created headwinds against Kraft Heinz, and their profit margin and market share were squeezed by private labels. Also, the cost cutting measures hurt their long term growth potential.

Kraft Heinz has been trying to find answers, and they will focus on emerging market growth and generating stable free cash flow. While I believe Kraft Heinz won't go anywhere as they are financially healthy and have many strong brands to rely on, I don't see any quick turnaround in site. It will be a long process of turning the ship around, and there is a possibility the profitable growth won't come. We shall see.

Woops with Occidental Petroleum

In April 2019, Berkshire Hathaway made a significant initial investment in Occidental Petroleum to assist in the acquisition of Anadarko Petroleum through preferred stock with warrants.

At that point, Warren Buffett thought the long-term growth of Permian Basin exploration and the U.S. oil production boom would make Occidental Petroleum a lot more valuable in the long run. That plan didn't really work out as Berkshire Hathaway wanted.

The Occidental Petroleum stock lost more than 70% of its value during the pandemic. The stock price recovered somewhat in 2022, but it started to decline again recently along with lower crude price. According to Dataroma, the average purchase price of Occidental Petroleum by Berkshire Hathaway is 13% higher than the current price.

I understand that Warren Buffett's favorite holding period is "Forever," but it's fair to say that the Occidental Petroleum investment has been disappointing so far. I don't expect the oil price recovery anytime soon with OPEC increasing their oil output.

As a substantial portion of Berkshire Hathaway's investment is a preferred stock with 8% dividend, Berkshire Hathaway would still make nice cash flow from the investment. However, the investment portfolio of Berkshire Hathaway will be negatively impacted by the lackluster performance of Occidental Petroleum for the short term.

Conclusion

Berkshire Hathaway stock is usually one of those investments that you just buy and forget about it, and you are very pleased when you remember to take a look a few years later. Several decades of data shows Berkshire Hathaway outperforming the benchmark year in and year out.

But even with a recent disappointing earnings report, I believe the long term growth trajectory of Berkshire Hathaway stock price is unchanged. They will be just fine.

It still is fun to analyze the rare mistakes by Warren Buffett and learn from what went wrong. It makes me feel better about myself knowing that even the legendary Warren Buffett makes a few mistakes once in a while.

Unfortunately, I expect Kraft Heinz and Occidental Petroleum will underperform the market in the short term, and Berkshire Hathaway portfolio will suffer from that.

In Occidental Petroleum's case, as oil price recovers, their stock price will somewhat recover as well. So there is some chance that the loss won't be too bad. In Kraft Heinz case, I believe the uphill is much steeper, and the turnaround plan will take several years to see some positive signs.

I'm interested to wait and see what Berkshire Hathaway will do with these two stocks.
